text: This video shows laparoscopic emergency myomectomy in case of severe bleeding on a previous myoma and after failure of medical treatment and blood transfusion.

The surgeon begins the myomectomy by controlling both uterine arteries at their origin. The assistant provides medial traction on the infundibulopelvic ligament, while the surgeon opens the peritoneum between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament to expand the retroperitoneal space.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum, allowing gas to facilitate dissection. The ureter is identified first, and dissection proceeds above it into the pararectal space, where the uterine artery is visible.

The surgeon uses traction and contraction movements to increase the space in front of the ureter, revealing the uterine artery at its origin. The uterus is rotated and visible below the forceps, with the pararectal fossa and umbilical artery also in view. The uterine artery is clipped at its origin as the uterus is moved medially.
The surgeon performs medial traction on the infundibulopelvic ligament using scissors held by the assistant, preparing to carry out the procedure on the opposite side.

The surgeon uses gas to dissect the retroperitoneal area, aiming to locate the ureter or umbilical artery. Short coagulation is applied to the connective tissue before cutting deeper into the retroperitoneal space.

The surgeon visually identifies the uterus and the uterine artery positioned just above and in front of the uterus. The connective tissue is being cut to improve visibility of the uterine artery, which is located deeper than usual. The uterine vein is visible below the uterine artery. The dissection of the uterine artery is being completed, emphasizing the importance of a bloodless laparoscopic myomectomy due to the patient's severe anemia.

The uterus is positioned under the scissors, with medial traction increasing the space between the uterus and the uterine artery. The uterine artery is clearly visible, and small connective tissue above it is coagulated. A clip is placed on the uterine artery for vascular control. The umbilical artery is seen lateral to the uterine artery, appearing enlarged due to the presence of a large myoma. The uterine artery is clipped safely at its origin under visual control.

The surgeon begins the myomectomy by performing a longitudinal hystereotomy using bipolar current, making a six-centimeter incision for the nine-centimeter myoma. The myoma, identified as having necrobiosis on MRI, is dissected from the healthy myometrium in a bloodless manner, with efforts to rapidly locate and extract the myoma.

The surgeon performs a smooth dissection of the myoma, applying bipolar current directly to the myoma while avoiding the healthy myometrium. The myoma is carefully separated from its bed in a bloodless manner, using traction and contraction techniques. The myoma is located close to the endometrium, requiring caution to prevent damage to the endometrial mucosa during dissection near the uterine cavity. Hydro-dissection may be used to assist in the complete removal of the myoma.

The surgeon places forceps at the edge of the myoma near the uterine cavity, carefully avoiding excessive energy use to prevent damage. The uterine cavity is opened, and the myoma is dissected without applying energy near the endometrium. Hemostasis is achieved through stitches rather than bipolar energy to minimize the risk of synechiae and preserve fertility.

The surgeon uses a 14mm needle with Vicryl suture to place stitches two to three centimeters lateral to the edge of the hystereotomy. The intracorporeal knotting technique is employed to achieve precise control over the suture tension and knot placement.

The surgeon observes good ischemia with no bleeding and applies three to four stitches for hemostasis on the nine-centimeter myoma. The technique involves invaginating the healthy myometrium into the uterine myoma bed, reducing the risk of abdominal adhesion between the uterus and bowel. Care is taken to avoid damaging the myometrium during the procedure.

The surgeon sutures the lower part of the hystereotomy using a Vicryl one suture, ensuring the needle is positioned on the opposite side from the previous stitch. Two or three knots are applied to achieve effective hemostasis of the myoma bed.

The surgeon places an additional stitch laterally on the upper part of the uterus for enhanced hemostasis due to the smooth nature of the large myoma. Care is taken to avoid incorporating the fallopian tube into the stitch, ensuring it is closed proximally. The knot is locked using an interrupted suture technique with Vicryl, resulting in a secure and efficient hemostasis. The final appearance of the suture is shown.

The surgeon places an additional lateral stitch to ensure better hemostasis due to myometrium retraction. A double flat knot is tied with good traction, taking care not to damage the healthy myometrium. These techniques aim to reduce the risk of intramyometrial hematoma and adhesions with the bowel, ensuring a smooth scar for future pregnancies.

The surgeon inserts an endoscopic bag into the abdominal cavity and places the excised myoma inside the bag for in-bag morcellation, aiming to reduce the risk of leomyomatosis or parasitic myoma formation.

After completing the morcellation of the myoma, the surgeon removes the endoscopic bag through the twelve millimeter trocar. The abdominal and pelvic cavity is thoroughly cleaned. The uterine arteries are inspected, with a clear view of the left uterine artery, ensuring there is no abnormal bleeding. The clip remains in place on the artery.
